extracted_information: The web content describes 'Upwelling,' an experimental editor designed to improve collaborative writing by integrating concepts from real-time collaboration and version control systems, particularly drawing parallels with Git workflows. It addresses shortcomings of existing tools like Google Docs (real-time collaboration's 'fishbowl effect') and traditional file-based systems (manual versioning, merging difficulties for prose). Upwelling introduces 'layers' and 'drafts' as core mechanisms for managing changes and facilitating review, aiming to provide flexibility for private work and deliberate collaboration, while borrowing heavily from software development's approach to version control. It highlights the need for both real-time and asynchronous collaboration, intelligent merging, and human-in-the-loop review.

technology_stack.collaboration_engine: Automerge CRDT (experimental fork with rich text formatting and author attribution)

specifications.data_storage: Uploads single tarball (stack + active drafts + metadata) to server for asynchronous collaboration; real-time uses Automerge sync protocol
specifications.file_export_import: Document export as file (via ?download URL), import by dragging into application

features.name: Combination of Real-time and Asynchronous Collaboration
features.description: Allows co-authors to collaborate in real-time when desired, but also supports private drafts. Within a draft, changes are merged in real-time; merging to the 'stack' (main document) is a deliberate, review-driven process. Addresses the 'fishbowl effect' of constant real-time visibility.

features.name: Layers and Drafts
features.description: Document built out of 'layers'; every edit occurs within one. Unmerged layers are 'drafts'. Drafts serve as a unit for grouping related changes (similar to Git commits/branches) and can be given titles to explain intent. Multiple drafts can exist simultaneously, separate from each other, allowing private work. Shared drafts enable real-time collaboration with cursor presence and author-specific highlighting.

features.name: Stack
features.description: The 'stack' contains the complete editing history of the document, starting from creation. Each merged layer is placed on top of the stack, representing a batch of changes relative to the previous layer. Allows navigating history and reviewing changes made by colleagues over time.

features.name: Floating Drafts / Automatic Rebasing
features.description: When one draft is merged onto the stack, all other active drafts are automatically updated to include those changes. This is analogous to Git's 'rebase' operation, ensuring drafts reflect the latest main document and surface potential conflicts early. Writers receive notifications when a draft is merged but can delay updating their current draft.

features.name: Always-on Change Tracking
features.description: Records the full history of the document automatically, allowing writers to decide retrospectively how much historic detail to display. This contrasts with tools requiring manual 'Track Changes' activation, which can be visually distracting.

features.name: Review and Merging
features.description: Reviewers can leave comments on drafts or edit them directly. Authors can 'merge' a draft into the stack when satisfied, making it a permanent, unmodifiable layer in the document's history. Relies on social conventions for review processes, rather than rigid software enforcement. Full attribution and history are preserved.

features.name: Conflict Management
features.description: Distinguishes between 'syntactic' conflicts (detectable by text structure, like two users changing the same word) and 'semantic' conflicts (inconsistent meaning, requiring human review). Focuses on enabling human navigation of conflicts rather than sophisticated automated resolution. Aims to reduce conflicts by encouraging communication, meaningful grouping of changes, and swift merging of small, focused drafts.

features.name: Simplified Change Visualization
features.description: Highlights insertions with a light background and marks deletions with a '➰' symbol, revealing deleted text on hover, to reduce visual noise compared to strikethrough text in other tools.

features.name: Granular Change Acceptance
features.description: Primary means for accepting/rejecting changes is per-draft. This allows grouping related changes into a single unit for review, rather than accepting/rejecting individual keystroke suggestions. Fine-grained adjustments within a draft are also possible.

features.name: Offline Mode
features.description: The web application works offline, allowing writers to continue working without an internet connection.

references.title: Visual Studio Live Share for real-time code reviews and interactive education
